title: The worrier part that runs catastrophic simulations all day
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The part that never stops calculating 

 You're trying to fall asleep. It's 2:13 AM. And your brain is running a full simulation of that awkward thing you...
htmlContent:
The part that never stops calculating

You're trying to fall asleep. It's 2:13 AM. And your brain is running a full simulation of that awkward thing you said at work six years ago. Then it jumps to a worst-case scenario about tomorrow. Then it imagines your partner getting into a car accident. Then it calculates the exact probability of losing your job, your home, your mind. All in about 90 seconds.

I know this part. I live with this part. It's exhausting. It's relentless. And it has a very specific job that it believes is keeping you alive.

Let's call it the worrier. Or the catastrophizer. Or the simulation engine. Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems (IFS), would call it a firefighter part - a protector that floods your system with worst-case scenarios to prevent you from being blindsided by actual disaster. Janina Fisher, who writes extensively about trauma-related parts, describes these as "preventive protectors" that try to manage your environment before anything bad can happen.

Here's the thing. This part isn't broken. It isn't stupid. It's doing exactly what it evolved to do.

The problem is that it's running on a system designed for saber-toothed tigers, and you're just trying to send an email.

Think about it. That part of you that runs catastrophic simulations all day? It's not trying to ruin your life. It's trying to predict every possible threat so you can prepare. So you can survive. So you're never caught off guard the way you were before.

Because something happened to you. Something that taught this part that the world is dangerous, that people can hurt you, that safety is an illusion. And now it's on high alert. Forever.

What the simulations are really trying to do

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how the nervous system is wired for threat detection. It's not a choice. It's a biological imperative. Your brainstem scans for danger 24/7, and when it finds a match - or even a possible match - it sends alarm signals throughout your body.

But here's what's wild. The worrier part doesn't just respond to real threats. It responds to imagined threats. Hypothetical threats. Threats that exist only in a future that hasn't happened yet.

Stay with me here. Because this is where it gets personal.

That simulation you're running right now about what might go wrong? It's not random. It's not meaningless. It's a desperate attempt by a protective part to give you control in a world that feels uncontrollable. If you can imagine every possible disaster, maybe you can prevent one. Maybe you can brace yourself. Maybe you can be ready this time.

I remember a period in my life when I couldn't stop running simulations. Every conversation I was about to have, I'd rehearse 17 different versions. Every trip I planned, I'd imagine the car breaking down, the plane crashing, the hotel being a disaster. I was mentally living in a thousand parallel universes, all of them terrible.

And you know what? It never helped. Not once. All that preparation didn't prevent a single bad thing from happening. It just made me exhausted and anxious and disconnected from the actual moment I was living in.

But that part didn't know that. It was just trying to protect me the only way it knew how.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. He describes how these protective parts develop in response to chronic trauma, and how they can be understood rather than fought. I found it helpful because it didn't tell me to just "stop worrying." It explained why my brain was doing this in the first place.

Why fighting the worrier makes it worse

Here's the trap most of us fall into. We try to argue with the worrier part. We tell it to shut up. We try to logic our way out of catastrophic thinking. We say things like "that's irrational" or "you're being dramatic" or "nothing bad is going to happen."

And guess what happens? The worrier part gets louder. More desperate. More creative with its scenarios.

Because when you fight a protector part, it has to escalate. It has to prove its value. It has to show you just how dangerous the world really is, because you clearly don't get it.

I learned this the hard way. For years, I tried to beat my worrier part into submission. I meditated. I journaled. I did breathing exercises. And every time, the catastrophizing got worse. Because I was treating the part like an enemy instead of a terrified internal being that needed to be heard.

Gabor Mate, who has spent decades studying trauma and addiction, often says that the question isn't "what's wrong with you?" but "what happened to you?" The same applies to our parts. The worrier isn't broken. It's responding to a history that made it necessary.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz. It walks you through the IFS model in a way that's accessible and compassionate. I read it slowly, one chapter at a time, because some of it hit close to home.

How to actually be with the worrier part

So if fighting doesn't work, what does?

I'm not going to give you a tidy three-step solution. That would be disrespectful to the complexity of what you're dealing with. But I can share what I've found helpful, and what many trauma-informed therapists recommend.

First, you have to recognize that this part exists for a reason. It's not a glitch. It's not a defect. It's a protector that has been working overtime, probably since you were very young. Thank it. Acknowledge its effort. You don't have to agree with its methods, but you can appreciate its intention.

Second, you can get curious instead of combative. When the catastrophic simulation starts running, try saying to yourself: "Oh, there's that part again. It's really scared right now. I wonder what it's afraid would happen if it stopped."

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up? Did your chest tighten? Did you want to dismiss this as too simple? That's your protector parts reacting. That's okay. Just notice it.

Third, you can ground yourself in the present moment. This isn't toxic positivity. It's not pretending everything is fine. It's simply noticing that right now, in this actual moment, you are not being attacked by a tiger. You are reading words on a screen. You are breathing. You are here.

Dan Siegel, who developed the concept of the "window of tolerance," talks about how trauma narrows our ability to stay present. The worrier part is trying to keep you within that narrow window by predicting threats before they arrive. But the paradox is that this actually widens the window. By being with the part instead of fighting it, you expand your capacity to tolerate uncertainty.

Right? Think about that. The very thing you're trying to escape - the catastrophic thinking - might actually be the doorway into greater resilience. But only if you stop trying to push it away.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. I mention it here because the worrier part often has terrible boundaries. It doesn't know when to stop. It doesn't know that it's not its job to control the future. Learning to set internal boundaries with this part - saying "I hear you, but I'm going to be present now" - can be .

The paradox of letting the worrier exist

Here's what I've come to believe, and I want you to sit with this.

The worrier part might never go away completely. And that's okay.

I know that's not what you wanted to hear. I know you wanted me to tell you how to make it stop. But I've spent years trying to make parts of myself go away, and it never worked. What worked was learning to coexist. Learning to say "I see you, I hear you, and I'm going to make the choice that's right for me right now."

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma survivors often develop these protective strategies that outlive their usefulness. The key isn't to eliminate them. It's to understand their origin and integrate them into a larger sense of self.

Your worrier part has been carrying a heavy load for a long time. It's been standing guard while you tried to sleep. It's been running simulations while you tried to enjoy a meal. It's been scanning for danger while you tried to connect with people you love.

It's tired. And it doesn't know how to stop.

But you can start a different relationship with it. You can begin to say "thank you for trying to protect me. I know you're scared. And I'm going to handle this from here."

Some days that will work. Some days it won't. Some days the worrier will be too loud and too convinced that disaster is imminent. On those days, just survive. Just breathe. Just get through the next five minutes.

This isn't a linear process. It's not a journey with a clear destination. It's a daily practice of showing up with curiosity and compassion for the parts of yourself that are trying so hard to keep you safe.

Even when their methods are exhausting.

Even when they keep you up at night.

Even when you wish they would just. Shut. Up.

They're not your enemy. They're just parts. And parts can change.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab (paid link)
